how.","sameAs":["https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/","https:\/\/www.facebook.com\/rippedbodycom","https:\/\/www.instagram.com\/andy_rippedbody\/","https:\/\/andymorgan.com\/","https:\/\/www.amazon.com\/Andy-John-Morgan\/e\/B07Q2J5ST6\/ref=ntt_dp_epwbk_2","https:\/\/x.com\/https:\/\/twitter.com\/RippedBodyjp","https:\/\/muscleandstrengthpyramids.com\/","https:\/\/soundcloud.com\/andyrippedbodyjp","morganandrew"]},{"@type":"Question","@id":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-count-macros\/#faq-question-1596705898305","position":1,"url":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-count-macros\/#faq-question-1596705898305","name":"How do I figure out my macros?","answerCount":1,"acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"Use a macro calculator, like <a href=\"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/macro-calculator\/\">The RippedBody Macro Calculator<\/a>, to figure out your macros for your body type and goals in 60 seconds.","inLanguage":"en-US"},"inLanguage":"en-US"},{"@type":"Question","@id":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-count-macros\/#faq-question-1596705909980","position":2,"url":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-count-macros\/#faq-question-1596705909980","name":"How do I calculate my macros for weight loss?","answerCount":1,"acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"Just use my <a href=\"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/macro-calculator\/\">macro calculator<\/a>.","inLanguage":"en-US"},"inLanguage":"en-US"},{"@type":"Question","@id":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-count-macros\/#faq-question-1596705919771","position":3,"url":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-count-macros\/#faq-question-1596705919771","name":"What is a macro?","answerCount":1,"acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"Macros refer to the three macronutrients: <strong>carbohydrate, protein, and fat<\/strong>.<br\/><br\/>Calorie intake determines whether weight is gained or lost, but the macronutrient content of the calories you eat has a significant effect on whether that change is fat or muscle mass, how you feel, perform, and how easy your nutrition plan is to stick to. This guide teaches you how to make meals out of your macros once you have <a href=\"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/macro-calculator\/\">calculated<\/a> them.","inLanguage":"en-US"},"inLanguage":"en-US"},{"@type":"Question","@id":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-count-macros\/#faq-question-1596705931993","position":4,"url":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-count-macros\/#faq-question-1596705931993","name":"What do you eat on a macro diet?","answerCount":1,"acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"A macro diet should be rich in fruits, vegetables, have sufficient protein (<a href=\"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/macros\/\">1 g\/lb of body weight per day<\/a>), and be predominantly fresh and unprocessed foods. It is the same as any other \"healthy\" diet; the difference is that people have daily carbohydrate, protein, and fat intake targets which affect the specific food choices. This guide is here to teach you how to build meals out of your macros.","inLanguage":"en-US"},"inLanguage":"en-US"},{"@type":"Question","@id":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-count-macros\/#faq-question-1596705940978","position":5,"url":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-count-macros\/#faq-question-1596705940978","name":"What should I do if I can't count macros?","answerCount":1,"acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"It’s possible to get shredded without counting anything if you are prepared to repeat the same meal combinations. I had two very successful cuts before I knew what I was doing, using that method. Here’s how it’s done:<br\/><br\/>• Cook most of your meals at home. Be sure you are consuming protein at each meal.<br\/>• Create a schedule based on your meal preference and stick to it.<br\/>• Track your body measurements and ensure your data is moving downwards.<br\/>• If you need to make a reduction, just remove a fist-sized portion of carbohydrates from your food each day. This should keep the scale moving in the right direction.<br\/>• Each time the fat loss stalls, remove another half-fist of carbohydrates from your meals. Do this as few times as you can, though, so that you’re eating as much as possible while still losing fat.<br\/><br\/><br\/><br\/>Using this method, technically your fat intake will remain pretty much unchanged throughout your cut, which isn’t optimal – carbs are important to fuel your training.","inLanguage":"en-US"},"inLanguage":"en-US"},{"@type":"Question","@id":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-count-macros\/#faq-question-1596706111152","position":6,"url":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-count-macros\/#faq-question-1596706111152","name":"How important is macro timing?","answerCount":1,"acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"Far less than was once thought. I have you covered in my article on <a href=\"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/nutrient-timing\/\">Nutrition Timing<\/a>.","inLanguage":"en-US"},"inLanguage":"en-US"},{"@type":"Question","@id":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-count-macros\/#faq-question-1596706126337","position":7,"url":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-count-macros\/#faq-question-1596706126337","name":"Why is it safe to not count most vegetables?","answerCount":1,"acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"Vegetables are not very energy-dense. With the exception of the few starchy ones (such as potatoes and carrots), it's tough to eat so many that it makes a significant impact on your calorie intake.<br\/><br\/><em>•<\/em> 100 g<em> of raw tomatoes: ~3 g carbs, 1 g protein, ~=16 kcal<\/em><br\/>• <em>100 g of spinach: ~3 g carbs, 2 g protein, ~=20 kcal<\/em><br\/>• <em><strong>vs<\/strong><\/em> <em>100 g of butter =100 g fat, ~= 900 kcal<\/em><br\/><br\/>Even if you choose to eat a truly huge amount of vegetables each day to keep yourself full, 1.5 kg\/3.3 lbs for example, and choose not to count any of it, at the worst case we're only talking a ~300 kcal increase above what you were counting - if your digestive system were as efficient as a cow that is.<br\/><br\/>In reality, the energy availability of that veg will be lower than the standard 4 kcal per 1 g for carbs because it will be mostly fiber, which our bodies are not very good at taking the energy from. Also, it's likely that your gut won't be able to handle such a high amount of fiber anyway, and the severe bloating and\/or diarrhea will get you to limit yourself naturally.<br\/><br\/>In a nutshell, fibrous (non-starchy) vegetables aren't something we need to worry about counting. (My<br\/><a href=\"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/faq\/\">fiber and intake guidelines<\/a>.)","inLanguage":"en-US"},"inLanguage":"en-US"},{"@type":"Question","@id":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-count-macros\/#faq-question-1596706246371","position":8,"url":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-count-macros\/#faq-question-1596706246371","name":"Are large swings in calorie intake day to day fine if they lead to the same average intake over time?","answerCount":1,"acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"No. The bodyweight change would be the same in both situations, however, this isn't optimal for workout recovery or nutrient partitioning. This is why binge-starve cycles don't lead to ripped physiques.","inLanguage":"en-US"},"inLanguage":"en-US"},{"@type":"Question","@id":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-count-macros\/#faq-question-1596706288304","position":9,"url":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-count-macros\/#faq-question-1596706288304","name":"What about [insert food here]?","answerCount":1,"acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"Look it up in a calculator, make an executive decision on what you deem to be a reasonable simplification for this food, stick to it and that way you have the 'consistency rule' covered.","inLanguage":"en-US"},"inLanguage":"en-US"},{"@type":"Question","@id":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-count-macros\/#faq-question-1596706318349","position":10,"url":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-count-macros\/#faq-question-1596706318349","name":"I prefer to count everything. Is that ok?","answerCount":1,"acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"That can be absolutely fine.   // Helper function to read a cookie value
  function getCookie(name) {
    var value = "; " + document.cookie;
    var parts = value.split("; " + name + "=");
    if (parts.length == 2) return parts.pop().split(";").shift();
  }

  // Helper function to set a cookie
  function setCookie(name, value, days) {
    var expires = "";
    if (days) {
      var date = new Date();
      date.setTime(date.getTime() + (days*24*60*60*1000));
      expires = "; expires=" + date.toUTCString();
    }
    document.cookie = name + "=" + (value || "")  + expires + "; path=/";
  }

  // Function to update display eligibility based on the time gap
  function updateDisplayEligibility(waitTimeSeconds) {
    setTimeout(function() {
      window.optinMonsterDisplayEligible = 'true';
      console.log('updateDisplayEligibility - OptinMonster is now display eligible');
    }, waitTimeSeconds * 1000); // Convert seconds to milliseconds
  }

  document.addEventListener('om.Campaign.show', function(event) {
    // console.log('om.Campaign.show', event.detail.Campaign);

    var now = new Date().getTime();
    setCookie('optinMonsterLastDisplayTimestamp', now, 1); // Store timestamp in cookie for 1 day
    window.optinMonsterDisplayEligible = 'false';

    updateDisplayEligibility(minimumDisplayGap);
  });

  var now = new Date().getTime();
  var lastDisplayTime = parseInt(getCookie('optinMonsterLastDisplayTimestamp')) || 0;
  var timePassed = (now - lastDisplayTime) / 1000; // Convert milliseconds to seconds

  if (timePassed >= minimumDisplayGap) {
    window.optinMonsterDisplayEligible = 'true';
    console.log('OptinMonster display eligible on page load');
  } else {
    var waitTimeSeconds = minimumDisplayGap - timePassed;
    window.optinMonsterDisplayEligible = 'false';
    updateDisplayEligibility(waitTimeSeconds);
  }</script> <style id="uagb-style-conditional-extension">@media (min-width: 1025px){body .uag-hide-desktop.uagb-google-map__wrap,body .uag-hide-desktop{display:none !important}}@media (min-width: 768px) and (max-width: 1024px){body .uag-hide-tab.uagb-google-map__wrap,body .uag-hide-tab{display:none !important}}@media (max-width: 767px){body .uag-hide-mob.uagb-google-map__wrap,body .uag-hide-mob{display:none !important}}</style><style id="uagb-style-frontend-62682">.uagb-heading-text mark {

.formkit-column{padding:55px 60px 55px 45px;}} .formkit-form[data-uid="8462769b72"] .formkit-header{font-size:14px !important;}.formkit-form[data-uid="8462769b72"] .formkit-input{border:1px solid #555555 !important;}.formkit-form[data-uid="8462769b72"] .formkit-submit{border:1px solid #333333 !important;}</style></form><div id="modal-ready"><p>Those serious about their strength and physique goals don’t only count calories; they pay close attention to what macros these calories come from (carbs, fat, and protein) because it leads to significantly better results.</p><p>The problem is that while using a <a href="https://rippedbody.com/macro-calculator/">macro calculator</a> is quick and easy, counting macros and making meals out of them can be a pain.</p><p>Ask yourself, it worth the extra mental headache of counting uncooked rice as 73 g of carbs per 100 g, or will simplifying it to 70 g suffice? I would say that it’s well worth going with the simplification and it’s this way of counting that I’ll teach you in this guide.</p><p>The content is based on what I’ve learned guiding clients over the last decade — real people with busy lives, not fitness professionals who can dedicate all their time to this stuff. Here’s what we’ll cover:</p><ol class="wp-block-list"><li><a href="#problem">The Problem With Macro Counting</a></li><li><a href="#consistency">The Importance of Consistency When You Count</a></li><li><a href="#own-rules">How To Make Your Own Simplified Counting Rules</a></li><li><a href="#rules">Simplified Macro Counting Recommendations</a></li><li><a href="#flexibility">Using Flexible Macronutrient Targets for Easier Weight Loss</a></li><li><a href="#meals">How To Make Meal Plans Out of Your Macros</a></li><li><a href="#eating-out">How To Count Macros When Eating Out</a></li><li><a href="#faq">Macro Counting FAQ</a></li></ol><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity is-style-wide"><h2 class="wp-block-heading has-text-align-center" id="problem">The Problem With Macro Counting</h2><p>Before we begin, I’d like to point out that I don’t believe the majority of the general population should try to count their macros as the first step to getting in shape. There are <a href="https://rippedbody.com/before-you-count/">much easier-to-implement lifestyle changes most people can make to get results</a>.</p><p>(Eating more fruit and vegetables, cutting out desserts, getting more exercise, cutting down on alcohol, getting sufficient sleep, etc.)</p><p>However, past a certain point, it becomes necessary to count calories and macros drive further change. But the majority of people do not think about the nutrition labels on foods until they attempt to do so. And when they do, it can be overwhelming.</p><p>Let’s consider Freddie, who we met in my <a href="/free-nutrition-course/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">Nutrition Setup Guide.</a> He’s just calculated that he needs to consume 180 g of protein, 40 g of fat, and 185 g of carbs each day. <em>How will he do that?</em></p><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized"><img decoding="async" width="1024" height="515" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/But-how-do-I-make-meals-out-of-my-macro-calculations-1024x515.png" alt="But, how do I make meals out of my macro calculations?" class="wp-image-59644" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/But-how-do-I-make-meals-out-of-my-macro-calculations-1024x515.png 1024w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/But-how-do-I-make-meals-out-of-my-macro-calculations-450x226.png 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/But-how-do-I-make-meals-out-of-my-macro-calculations-768x386.png 768w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/But-how-do-I-make-meals-out-of-my-macro-calculations-1536x772.png 1536w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/But-how-do-I-make-meals-out-of-my-macro-calculations-1568x788.png 1568w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/But-how-do-I-make-meals-out-of-my-macro-calculations.png 1779w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/But-how-do-I-make-meals-out-of-my-macro-calculations-450x226@2x.png 900w" sizes="(max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px"><figcaption class="wp-element-caption"><em>“But, how do I make meals out of my macro calculations?!”</em></figcaption></figure><p>He’ll probably walk into the supermarket that evening and start grabbing food items to look at what calories and macros are in them.</p><p>He’ll see that the majority of foods that he knows should make up the large part of his diet (fresh fruits, vegetables, meat, fish, and some dairy products) do not come with nutrition labels. It’s all the delicious shit in boxes and packets in the middle of the supermarket that have the labels, but he knows that building a diet that way isn’t going to be best for his long-term health.</p><p>Shit.</p><p>“Can someone just give me a meal plan?” Freddie screams. And sure enough, our phones are listening, and he’s served an advert delivering him that later in the day.</p><p>He buys but is bored after a month. He feels like a slave to the meal plan, not knowing how to navigate his nutrition targets without it. He’s stopped going out with friends and he won’t allow himself to have a slice of pizza at a party because “it’s not in the plan.”</p><p>Something has to change. He realizes that he has to learn to build meals out of his favorite foods that fit his nutrition targets if he’s going to reach his physique goals. He can go two routes from here:</p><ol class="wp-block-list"><li>Log everything you eat into a nutrition calculator every day.</li><li>Use simplified rules to help make building meals you love easier, and then rotate them across the week.</li></ol><p>The first method can drive people nuts as they seek perfection, so it’s the second method that I recommend. But only after a period of logging everything. Why? Because people are horrible at estimating <a href="https://rippedbody.com/calories/" data-wpil-monitor-id="130">calorie intake</a>.</p><h3 class="wp-block-heading">Log Everything You Eat And Drink For Two Weeks So That Your  Calorie Counting Doesn’t Go Horribly Wrong</h3><p>The <a href="https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/1454084/">research on calorie counting</a> shows that people are terrible at it. Under-reporting is rife.</p><p>I’ve lost count of the number of times that someone has told me that they are eating [<em>insert low-calorie number here</em>] per day, and yet they <a href="https://rippedbody.com/eating-more-than-you-think/">can’t lose weight</a>. Sure, they might need to reduce calories further, but the chances are they are just screwing something up with the way they are counting.</p><p>Here are some common things people forget to count:</p><ul class="wp-block-list"><li>The 40 g of fat in your salad dressing. (350 kcal)</li><li>The calories from the cola and fruit juices.</li><li>The two large-pour whiskeys you have every night. (250 kcal)</li><li>The 30 g of butter you put into your coffee every morning because you’ve been tricked into believing that this “gives you energy” and helps burn more body fat. (270 kcal)</li></ul><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="1024" height="732" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Under-reporting-macro-intake-is-common-1024x732.png" alt="Under-reporting macro intake is common" class="wp-image-59646" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Under-reporting-macro-intake-is-common-1024x732.png 1024w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Under-reporting-macro-intake-is-common-450x322.png 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Under-reporting-macro-intake-is-common-768x549.png 768w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Under-reporting-macro-intake-is-common-1536x1098.png 1536w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Under-reporting-macro-intake-is-common-1568x1121.png 1568w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Under-reporting-macro-intake-is-common.png 1690w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Under-reporting-macro-intake-is-common-450x322@2x.png 900w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px"><figcaption class="wp-element-caption">Under-reporting macro intake is exceptionally common.</figcaption></figure><p>To prevent this from happening, I would recommend that you use an online calorie and macro counter to <em>log every single thing except water that you put in your mouth for an entire week</em>. You’ll need to buy a small electric food scale to do this.</p><p><strong>I’d recommend you use an app called <a href="https://macrofactorapp.com/macrofactor/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">MacroFactor</a>.</strong> It’s the largest <em>verified</em> nutrition database available, so it doesn’t rely on the user-generated (and often flawed) inputs that many other apps do. <em><em><mark style="background-color:#fffeb3" class="has-inline-color">(Enter code <strong>“ripped”</strong> for an extended two-week free trial.)</mark></em></em></p><p>Try not to change your diet. What you discover may be enlightening.</p><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity is-style-wide"><h2 class="wp-block-heading has-text-align-center" id="consistency">The Importance of Consistency When You Count</h2><p>It’s important to acknowledge that no matter how accurate we <em>attempt</em> to be with how we count our calorie and macro intake, we will never be perfect — there are food labeling errors and the estimations built into the nutrition calculators we use.</p><p>This is such a critical point that I’ll say that again:</p><p><strong>There <em>will</em> be a difference between what you <em>think</em> you are eating and what you are <em>actually</em> eating each day.</strong></p><p>Nutrition calculators are great to show us if we are making any large errors, but the numbers can give us a false sense of precision.</p><p>Instead of fighting this inevitable inaccuracy, we can embrace it by making simplified counting rules which make our lives easier.</p><p>So for example, instead of counting 100g of dry pasta as <em>73 g carbs, 2 g fat, and 15 g protein</em>, we might choose to just count it as 70 g of carbs and 15 g of protein.</p><p>The only important thing then is that we are consistent with how we count. So for example, we don’t want to count our rice uncooked the one day and cooked the next, and we don’t want to swap between the values given in different calculators from day-to-day.</p><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="1024" height="770" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/What-happens-to-macro-counting-accuracy-as-we-make-simplifications-1024x770.png" alt="What happens to macro counting accuracy as we make simplifications" class="wp-image-59647" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/What-happens-to-macro-counting-accuracy-as-we-make-simplifications-1024x770.png 1024w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/What-happens-to-macro-counting-accuracy-as-we-make-simplifications-450x338.png 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/What-happens-to-macro-counting-accuracy-as-we-make-simplifications-768x578.png 768w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/What-happens-to-macro-counting-accuracy-as-we-make-simplifications-1536x1155.png 1536w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/What-happens-to-macro-counting-accuracy-as-we-make-simplifications-1568x1179.png 1568w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/What-happens-to-macro-counting-accuracy-as-we-make-simplifications.png 1606w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/What-happens-to-macro-counting-accuracy-as-we-make-simplifications-450x338@2x.png 900w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px"><figcaption class="wp-element-caption">What happens to calorie and macro counting accuracy as we make simplifications.</figcaption></figure><p>As we build up simplifications in our diet, we may end up a little over or under our target intake. You might think you’re consuming 1800 kcal, but you actually consuming 2160 kcal because of the simplifications. Does this matter? I’d argue not. Because as long as you are consistent, you can adjust things upwards or downwards relative to your intake baseline to get the <a href="https://rippedbody.com/results/">desired result</a>.</p><ul class="wp-block-list"><li>If you are losing weight too fast, <em>increase</em> calories.</li><li>If you are losing weight too slowly, <em>decrease</em> calories.</li></ul><p>I know that’s not rocket science, but it’s something many folks seem to miss. They make new calorie calculations instead of making a simple <a href="https://rippedbody.com/how-to-adjust-macros/">macro adjustment</a>.</p><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity is-style-wide"><h2 class="wp-block-heading has-text-align-center" id="own-rules">How To Make Your Own Counting Rules</h2><p>Before I get into some examples of simplified macro counting recommendations, I’d like to show you how I’ve made them, so that you know how to make them on your own.</p><figure class="wp-block-image alignright"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="333" height="501" src="/wp-content/uploads/2014/10/Dry-Macaroni-Nutritional-Info.jpg" alt="Dry Macaroni Nutritional Info" class="wp-image-35357" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2014/10/Dry-Macaroni-Nutritional-Info.jpg 333w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2014/10/Dry-Macaroni-Nutritional-Info-299x450.jpg 299w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 333px) 100vw, 333px"></figure><p>Have a look at this label for 100 g of dried pasta. Here are some examples of simplified rules we can make:</p><ul class="wp-block-list"><li>100g of dry pasta ~= <em>70 g carbs, 0 g fat, and 0 g protein.</em></li><li>100g of dry pasta ~= <em>70 g carbs, 0 g fat, and 10 g protein.</em></li><li>100g of dry pasta ~= <em>75 g carbs, 0 g fat, and 10 g protein.</em></li><li>100g of dry pasta ~= <em>75 g carbs, 0 g fat, and 15 g protein.</em></li><li>100g of dry pasta ~= <em>75 g carbs, 2 g fat, and 15 g protein.</em></li></ul><p><strong>Actual:</strong> 100g of dry pasta = <em>73 g carbs, 2 g fat, and 15 g protein.</em></p><p>In all but one of the above examples, calories will be underestimated. You’ll see that they range from least to most accurate top-down, with the last barely being a counting simplification at all.</p><p>How accurate you wish to be with your rules is up to you. The trade-off to greater accuracy is more complication.</p><p>I’d say that it’s better to underestimate because fat is slow to gain but can be quickly burned off, whereas muscle takes a lot of effort to gain, but can easily be lost if we lost weight too quickly.</p><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="1024" height="770" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-acceptable-range-of-calorie-and-macro-counting-accuracy-1024x770.png" alt="The acceptable range of calorie and macro counting accuracy" class="wp-image-59645" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-acceptable-range-of-calorie-and-macro-counting-accuracy-1024x770.png 1024w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-acceptable-range-of-calorie-and-macro-counting-accuracy-450x338.png 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-acceptable-range-of-calorie-and-macro-counting-accuracy-768x578.png 768w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-acceptable-range-of-calorie-and-macro-counting-accuracy-1536x1155.png 1536w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-acceptable-range-of-calorie-and-macro-counting-accuracy-1568x1179.png 1568w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-acceptable-range-of-calorie-and-macro-counting-accuracy.png 1606w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-acceptable-range-of-calorie-and-macro-counting-accuracy-450x338@2x.png 900w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px"><figcaption class="wp-element-caption">The acceptable range of calorie and macro counting accuracy.</figcaption></figure><p>The green area in the figure above represents the target area. Think about this when constructing your simplified rules. Be on the black line or slightly above.</p><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity is-style-wide"><p style="font-size:28px"><strong>BEFORE GOING ANY FURTHER…</strong></p><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="1024" height="633" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Nutrition-Setup-Guide-Device-Display-1024x633.png" alt="The RippedBody Nutrition Setup Guide - Device Display" class="wp-image-59287" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Nutrition-Setup-Guide-Device-Display-1024x633.png 1024w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Nutrition-Setup-Guide-Device-Display-450x278.png 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Nutrition-Setup-Guide-Device-Display-768x475.png 768w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Nutrition-Setup-Guide-Device-Display.png 1400w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Nutrition-Setup-Guide-Device-Display-450x278@2x.png 900w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px"></figure><p><strong>May I send you our free Transformation Kickstart Course and nutrition ebook?</strong></p><p>People consistently make the same simple mistakes when setting up their diets. My free 15-lesson email course will help you avoid the most common ones (including training mistakes too). !important;}</style></form></div><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity is-style-wide"><h2 class="wp-block-heading has-text-align-center" id="rules">Simplified Macro Counting Recommendations</h2><p>In this section, I will make some suggestions on simplified counting rules you can use.</p><p><strong>Everything below is a simplification for raw (or uncooked) foods</strong>.</p><p>This is because the weight of food differs depending on how you cook it and how long you cook it. This is because water is gained or lost through the cooking process. If you cook a steak for too long, it loses water and weighs less. The fat and protein content is the same, but it weighs less. Similarly, if you cook rice or pasta for too long, it goes mushy because it holds more water and weighs more. Therefore, I recommend you weigh your food before you cook it.</p><p>If something is in a packet with the nutrition information label on it, look at the macro content and make your own simplified rule if you’re going to eat it often. Most things will be listed ‘per 100g’, others will be ‘per serving.’ Make sure you double-check and don’t assume that it’s to the nearest 100g always, or you’ll get caught out.</p><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity"><h3 class="wp-block-heading has-text-align-center">How to Count Carbs</h3><p class="has-text-align-center"><em><strong>1 g Carbohydrate = ~4 kcal</strong></em></p><p>Carbs are going to come through your diet in a variety of sources: fruit, starchy carbs, veg, and in the other things you don’t generally think about like dairy, sauces, and drinks.</p><h4 class="wp-block-heading">Starchy carbs</h4><p>These will form the bulk of your carb intake. (Think bread, rice, pasta, potatoes.)</p><ul class="wp-block-list"><li>Raw potatoes: ~15 g carbs per 100 g weight.</li><li>Sweet potatoes: ~25 g per 100 g weight.</li><li>Dried rice: ~70 g of carbs per 100 g weight. This works for most dried pasta too.</li><li>Bread: varies (some manufacturers add a lot of butter for flavor). Look at the nutrition label if available or in one of the nutrition calculators.</li></ul><p>You’ll see that I’ve ignored protein and fat content in the starchy carbs. That’s purposeful to make things easier, but it’s up to you.</p><p><strong>Pro tip: </strong>Microwavable rice and other similar things won’t conform to the simplifications above because they are partially cooked and have greater water content.</p><h4 class="wp-block-heading">Fruit</h4><ul class="wp-block-list"><li>You may consider one ‘medium’ sized piece of fruit (an apple, a banana, a pear, an orange, etc.) to be 25 g of carbs.</li><li>For other fruits, weigh them once and look them up.</li><li>If you are unsure or hate the idea of ‘medium,’ weigh it once and look it up.</li></ul><p><strong>Pro tip:</strong> Avoid smoothies and fruit juice when dieting. They have all the sugar, but none of the fiber, so they are easy to consume but not very filling.</p><h4 class="wp-block-heading">Vegetables</h4><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="800" height="800" src="/wp-content/uploads/2014/07/ALMOST-CALORIE-FREE-VEGGIES-RPB-Site-Version.jpg" alt="" class="wp-image-49683" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2014/07/ALMOST-CALORIE-FREE-VEGGIES-RPB-Site-Version.jpg 800w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2014/07/ALMOST-CALORIE-FREE-VEGGIES-RPB-Site-Version-150x150.jpg 150w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2014/07/ALMOST-CALORIE-FREE-VEGGIES-RPB-Site-Version-450x450.jpg 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2014/07/ALMOST-CALORIE-FREE-VEGGIES-RPB-Site-Version-768x768.jpg 768w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2014/07/ALMOST-CALORIE-FREE-VEGGIES-RPB-Site-Version-150x150@2x.jpg 300w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 800px) 100vw, 800px"><figcaption class="wp-element-caption"><em>For more useful graphics, check out my <a rel="noreferrer noopener" href="https://www.instagram.com/andy_rippedbody/" target="_blank">Instagram</a>.</em></figcaption></figure><ul class="wp-block-list"><li>You can see from the image below that leafy green vegetables have a low calorie amount per cup. For this reason, I feel it’s fine to simply not count them towards your targets. It’s not that the calories in these vegetables don’t have energy, it’s that you’re purposefully choosing not to count them. I consider a reasonable amount to apply this rule to as 2-4 cups per day.</li><li>Count starchy vegetables, as they are more energy-dense. Examples: carrots, peas, corn, potatoes, parsnips. (When looking these up, you’ll see that the energy content is relatively high for a vegetable, and fiber content per gram of carbs is low.)</li><li>For anything else, look it up, make your own rule, stick to that rule. I’ll cover how to make your own simplified rules in the next section.</li></ul><p><strong>Carbs in other things that add up quickly and are easily missed:</strong></p><ul class="wp-block-list"><li>Drinks (milk, juice, soft-drinks)</li><li>Dairy</li><li>Protein powder</li><li>Sauces</li><li>Salad dressings</li></ul><p>Check the packaging or look it up and count it against your daily target.</p><p><strong>Pro tip:</strong> If you come across something labeled as “net carbs”, I’d recommend you ignore it. It’s labeling trickery. Count all the carbs as carbs. More on this here: <a href="https://rippedbody.com/net-carbs/">Should I Count Net Carbs?</a></p><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity"><h3 class="wp-block-heading has-text-align-center">How to Count Protein</h3><p class="has-text-align-center"><em><strong>1 g Protein = ~4 kcal</strong></em></p><ul class="wp-block-list"><li>Uncooked beef/ chicken/ pork/ lamb/ fish 100 g = ~25 g of protein.</li><li>One large egg = ~8 g protein 5 g fat.</li><li>Egg whites = ~4 g protein.</li></ul><p>The fat content in protein sources can quickly add up, so I would recommend you look up the fat content for all. Some cuts of meats have fastly higher fat content than others. This can push you over your fat macro budget for the day very quickly.</p><p>Here are the leanest protein sources:</p><ul class="wp-block-list"><li>Chicken breast (skinless)</li><li>Red meats that visually lack much whiteness</li><li>Most white fish</li><li>Some cuts of tuna (again, those that lack whiteness)</li><li>Protein shakes</li><li>Skimmed milk and other low-fat dairy.</li></ul><p>To say that I am not a fan of companies that sell <a href="https://rippedbody.com/supplements/">supplements</a> would be an understatement. However, I do concede that in most countries in the world, the cheapest way to hit your protein requirements, aside from chicken breast, is protein powder. Consider this if you are on a budget.</p><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="800" height="800" src="/wp-content/uploads/2014/07/Get-more-protein-Site-Version.jpg" alt="" class="wp-image-49684" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2014/07/Get-more-protein-Site-Version.jpg 800w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2014/07/Get-more-protein-Site-Version-150x150.jpg 150w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2014/07/Get-more-protein-Site-Version-450x450.jpg 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2014/07/Get-more-protein-Site-Version-768x768.jpg 768w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2014/07/Get-more-protein-Site-Version-150x150@2x.jpg 300w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 800px) 100vw, 800px"><figcaption class="wp-element-caption"><em>For more useful graphics, check out my <a href="https://www.instagram.com/andy_rippedbody/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">Instagram</a>.</em></figcaption></figure><p><strong>Pro tip:</strong> The trade-off to drinking our food is that it is less satisfying, more easily digested, and we get hungry quicker than if it were eating regular food. Avoid liquid food when dieting. But for those bulking and struggling to get in enough calories to hit your targets, liquid foods like protein shakes or fruit juices can be helpful.</p><h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>PROTEIN SOURCES PER 100 CALORIES</strong></h4><p>If you struggle to get in enough protein while sticking to your calorie budget, this table from MacroFactor may be helpful.</p><figure obtained from the </em><a href="https://macrofactorapp.com/"><em>MacroFactor app</em></a><em> database.</em></p><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity"><h3 class="wp-block-heading has-text-align-center">How to Count Fat</h3><p class="has-text-align-center"><em><strong>1 g Fat = ~9 kcal</strong></em></p><p>Fat has the highest energy density of the macronutrients. I suggest that you consider counting the fat in <em>everything</em>.</p><p><strong><em>How many grams of fat are in that cut of steak? How about after it’s grilled and some fat has dripped off of it, should I weigh the fat and deduct from the total?</em></strong></p><p>Here’s the most sensible strategy — look it up in a nutrition calculator, make your best, educated guess at the fat content, and then forget about it. You’re likely to eat the same cuts of meat again and again, so it won’t matter because you’ll be following the ‘consistency rule.’</p><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity"><h3 class="wp-block-heading has-text-align-center">How to Count Alcohol</h3><p class="has-text-align-center"><em><strong>1 g Alcohol = ~7 kcal</strong></em></p><p>It is challenging to make simplifications for alcohol, especially beer, as each drink will vary. Here is a rough idea:</p><ul class="wp-block-list"><li>Beer @5%: ~150 kcal, ~12 g carbs, ~14 g alcohol (per 12 floz/350 ml can/bottle)</li><li>White wine @10%: ~200 kcal, ~7 g carbs, ~25 g alcohol (250 g glass, 1/3 bottle)</li><li>Red wine @10%: ~210 kcal, 9 g carbs~25 g alcohol</li><li>Spirit shot @40%: 70/84 kcal (25 ml/1 fl.oz)</li><li>You can look up your favorite beer (or other alcohol) <a rel="noreferrer noopener" href="https://www.calorieking.com/calories-in-beer.html" target="_blank">here</a>.</li></ul><p>Most generic spirits will be 40% alcohol with no other macro content. You take the amount you drink, multiply by the alcohol content, then multiply by the calories per gram of alcohol.</p><p>So if you have four European shots (25 ml), that’s 100 ml. 100×0.4×7 = 280 kcal. Deduct the carbs from your allowance for the day.</p><p>Alcohol isn’t part of your three macro targets, but it is going to count towards the daily calorie balance, which needs to be maintained, so <a href="/macros/">reduce your carb and fat intake accordingly</a>.</p><p>On the occasions where you may have many drinks, see my <a href="/alcohol/">alcohol guide</a>.</p><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="768" height="1024" src="/wp-content/uploads/2017/06/zubrowka-vodka-intake-guidelines-e1514977581866-768x1024.jpg" alt="Vodka intake guidelines for calculating calories" class="wp-image-46984" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/06/zubrowka-vodka-intake-guidelines-e1514977581866.jpg 768w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/06/zubrowka-vodka-intake-guidelines-e1514977581866-338x450.jpg 338w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/06/zubrowka-vodka-intake-guidelines-e1514977581866-576x768.jpg 576w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/06/zubrowka-vodka-intake-guidelines-e1514977581866-338x450@2x.jpg 676w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 768px) 100vw, 768px"><figcaption class="wp-element-caption">Zubrowka mixed with apple juice tastes awesome. Thank me later.</figcaption></figure><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity is-style-wide"><h2 class="wp-block-heading has-text-align-center" id="flexibility">Using Flexible Macronutrient Targets for Easier Weight Loss</h2><p>Ok, so we’ve made our lives a lot easier. We know we’re a little inaccurate, but we’re in the right range and we’re being consistent. Within the counting framework you’ve created to make life easier, are you now going to shit it all up by aiming to get <em>exactly</em> 67 g of fat, 173 g of protein, and 266 g of carbohydrate each day? I certainly hope not — this is exactly the all-or-nothing mindset we’re trying to avoid.</p><p>So this begs the question, <em>how accurate should we be?</em></p><p>I’d recommend that you <strong><em>aim to be within 10% either side of each macro target for the day, 90% of the time.</em></strong> (As long as the other 10% of the time you aren’t abusing it by binge eating.)</p><p>For those who are already <a href="https://rippedbody.com/body-fat-guide/">sub-10% body fat</a> and destined for the stage, tighten this up to 5% either side.</p><p>As much as you think your diet may be varied, the foods that you actually cook from and eat will not be that numerous, so it won’t take long to look everything up in one of the nutrition calculators I listed above once. (Just do it when you get home from the supermarket over the next week so that it isn’t a chore.)</p><p>Make a note of any new foods on a memo sheet (‘cheat sheet’) and pin it up on your fridge.</p><p>Put together a few meals out of your favorite foods, and put these meals together so that you have a set of meals for your training days and rest days that fit your macros, then rotate them.</p><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity is-style-wide"><h2 class="wp-block-heading has-text-align-center" id="meals">How to Make Meal Plans Out of Your Macros</h2><p>Creating meals we enjoy doesn’t have to be tedious or difficult, we just need to apply the simplified rules we learned above, and convert them into meals that we enjoy so we can rotate them throughout the week.</p><h3 class="wp-block-heading">Training Day Meal Examples</h3><p><strong>Assumptions:</strong></p><ul class="wp-block-list"><li>You have calculated your daily macros (using <a rel="noreferrer noopener" href="/complete-diet-nutrition-set-up-guide/" target="_blank">this guide</a>) to be: 175 g of protein, 250 g of carbs, and 60 g of fat.</li><li>You are going to train fasted in the morning (well, actually you’ll have a 25 g whey shake) and have two big meals for lunch and dinner, split 50-50. (More on timing options in <a rel="noreferrer noopener" href="/complete-diet-nutrition-set-up-guide/" target="_blank">that guide</a> also.)</li><li>Therefore, you need to make two meals, each with 75 g of protein, 125 g of carbs, and 30 g of fat.</li></ul><p>(Note: It doesn’t have to be an exact 50-50 split, but let’s just roll with this for the example.)</p><h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>Example lunch for a training day</strong></h4><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="892" height="1024" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/RippedBody-Meal-Examples-Protein-0001-892x1024.jpg" alt="" class="wp-image-58488" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/RippedBody-Meal-Examples-Protein-0001-892x1024.jpg 892w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/RippedBody-Meal-Examples-Protein-0001-892x1024-392x450.jpg 392w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/RippedBody-Meal-Examples-Protein-0001-892x1024-669x768.jpg 669w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/RippedBody-Meal-Examples-Protein-0001-892x1024-392x450@2x.jpg 784w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 892px) 100vw, 892px"></figure><p>What we have here is 300 g of shredded lean beef, 180 g of couscous, and a couple of fists of vegetables.</p><p><strong>Using our simplified rules, the math on this is simple:</strong></p><ul class="wp-block-list"><li>We need 75 g of protein. There is ~25g in 100 g of uncooked meat, so we need 300 g of meat. (75/25)*100</li><li>We need 125 g of carbs. There is 70g in 100 g of dry pasta/couscous, so we need 180 g of dry pasta/couscous. (125/70)*100</li><li>30 g of fat = Estimate 10 g of fat per 100 g of lean beef.</li></ul><p><strong>Note: </strong>Vegetables were grilled, so no fat has been added to the count. If you stir-fry these or add any oil to the cooking process, add that into your numbers. Fat can very easily be overlooked and will mess up your calculations if you don’t pay attention.</p><h4 class="wp-block-heading">Example dinner for a training day</h4><p>The first meal was easy enough, right? Let’s try the second meal of the day.</p><p>We still need the same quantity of protein, carbs, and fat. We’re going to choose a leaner cut of meat like turkey, rather than steak, as we only have 30g of fat to work with, and let’s say we want to feel really full with potatoes.</p><p>Here’s how that would look:</p><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="892" height="1024" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/RippedBody-Meal-Examples-Protein-0002-892x1024.jpg" alt="" class="wp-image-58487" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/RippedBody-Meal-Examples-Protein-0002-892x1024.jpg 892w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/RippedBody-Meal-Examples-Protein-0002-892x1024-392x450.jpg 392w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/RippedBody-Meal-Examples-Protein-0002-892x1024-669x768.jpg 669w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/RippedBody-Meal-Examples-Protein-0002-892x1024-392x450@2x.jpg 784w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 892px) 100vw, 892px"></figure><p>What we have here is 300 g of turkey steak, around 500 g of potatoes, and a couple of fists of vegetables.</p><p><strong>Using our simplified rules, the math on this one is also simple:</strong></p><ul class="wp-block-list"><li>We need 75 g of protein. There is ~25 g in 100 g of uncooked meat, so we need 300 g of meat. (75/25)*100</li><li>We need 100 g of carbs. There is ~20 g in 100 g of dry potatoes, so we need 500 g of dry potatoes (two medium or one very large one). (100/20)*100</li><li>We need 30 g of fat. Turkey is <em>very</em> lean, so we have created our rule of 0 g of fat per 100 g. The fat calculated here comes from a couple of tablespoons of oil used to cook both the mushrooms and the turkey.</li></ul><p><strong>Tip: </strong>Reduce the fat used for cooking if you are trying to lower your numbers in any meal.</p><h3 class="wp-block-heading">Rest Day Meal Examples</h3><p>Now, let’s say you are having a lower calorie day, with lower carbs and more fats — something like 175 g of protein, 75 g of carbs, and 80 g of fat.</p><p>What’s the simple adjustment you might make?</p><p>Well, just choose a slightly fattier protein, and then drop out the majority of your starchy carbohydrate.</p><p>You might begin to see a pattern here; the ‘star’ of the show is your protein portion, which we then complement with veggies, carbs & condiments, depending on our goals.</p><h4 class="wp-block-heading">Example meal for a rest day</h4><p>Boring salads? Here’s how a salad can look:</p><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="942" height="942" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/RippedBody-Meal-Examples-Salad-0003.jpg" alt="" class="wp-image-58486" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/RippedBody-Meal-Examples-Salad-0003.jpg 942w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/RippedBody-Meal-Examples-Salad-0003-150x150.jpg 150w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/RippedBody-Meal-Examples-Salad-0003-450x450.jpg 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/RippedBody-Meal-Examples-Salad-0003-768x768.jpg 768w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/RippedBody-Meal-Examples-Salad-0003-150x150@2x.jpg 300w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/RippedBody-Meal-Examples-Salad-0003-450x450@2x.jpg 900w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 942px) 100vw, 942px"></figure><p><strong>Let’s break those numbers down:</strong></p><ul class="wp-block-list"><li><strong>80 g of protein</strong> from 300 g of chicken breast, one whole egg, 30 g of bacon, plus the 15 g of cheddar cheese.</li><li><strong>0 g of carbs.</strong> Yes, there are almost 500 g of vegetables in this but, since it’s mostly greens, we make our lives easier by not trying to figure out how many grams of carbs are in lettuce, tomato, spinach, etc. You can choose to count them, of course, just be consistent with whatever you decide to do.</li><li><strong>45 g of fat</strong> from the tablespoon of olive oil used in the dressing, the fat in the egg, the 30 g of bacon, and the 15 g of cheddar cheese.</li></ul><p>Trust me when I say that you will be full after eating all that volume. When in doubt, remember:</p><ol class="wp-block-list"><li>Pick your protein.</li><li>Add veggies.</li><li>Condiments and seasoning for flavor.</li><li>Complete with carbs and/or fat, depending on your macros.</li></ol><p><strong>Do that with 6–8 different set meals you can rotate during the week and make it easy for yourself to hit your numbers.</strong></p><p>Then as you get more experience, add in some more meals.</p><p>Start small and simple, then build.</p><h4 class="wp-block-heading">Let’s look at what a day might look like rotating those meals</h4><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="892" height="1024" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/RippedBody-Whole-Day-Macros-892x1024.jpg" alt="" class="wp-image-58485" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/RippedBody-Whole-Day-Macros-892x1024.jpg 892w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/RippedBody-Whole-Day-Macros-892x1024-392x450.jpg 392w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/RippedBody-Whole-Day-Macros-892x1024-669x768.jpg 669w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/RippedBody-Whole-Day-Macros-892x1024-392x450@2x.jpg 784w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 892px) 100vw, 892px"></figure><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity is-style-wide"><h2 class="wp-block-heading has-text-align-center" id="eating-out">How To Count Macros When Eating Out</h2><p>We know that being perfect isn’t the goal, but trying to stay consistent when eating out can be a challenge, too.</p><p>Bear in mind that restaurants don’t care about your macros, and the chef usually wants you to enjoy delicious food so you recommend them and come back often. This means that restaurants tend to serve large portions and have a lot of hidden (but delicious fats). The average restaurant meal is high in calories.</p><p>You can still enjoy meals out whilst maintaining progress, you just have to be smart:</p><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="1024" height="1024" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/Portion-sizes-rippedbody-macro-wizard-1024x1024.jpg" alt="" class="wp-image-58484" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/Portion-sizes-rippedbody-macro-wizard-1024x1024.jpg 1024w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/Portion-sizes-rippedbody-macro-wizard-1024x1024-150x150.jpg 150w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/Portion-sizes-rippedbody-macro-wizard-1024x1024-450x450.jpg 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/Portion-sizes-rippedbody-macro-wizard-1024x1024-768x768.jpg 768w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/Portion-sizes-rippedbody-macro-wizard-1024x1024-150x150@2x.jpg 300w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/Portion-sizes-rippedbody-macro-wizard-1024x1024-450x450@2x.jpg 900w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px"><figcaption class="wp-element-caption">Image credit: <a rel="noreferrer noopener" href="https://themacrowizard.com/empieza-aqui" target="_blank">The Macro Wizard</a> & <a rel="noreferrer noopener" href="https://www.precisionnutrition.com/calorie-control-guide-infographic" target="_blank">Precision Nutrition.</a></figcaption></figure><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity"><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter wp-image-48688 size-medium"><img decoding="async" alt="" class="wp-image-48688" srcset="/wp-content/uploads/2017/11/Fatty-Meat-and-Veggies-450x338.jpg, /wp-content/uploads/2017/11/Fatty-Meat-and-Veggies-450x338@2x.jpg 2x"><figcaption class="wp-element-caption">Eating out can be easy to navigate if you apply simple rules. Be wary of fatty cuts of meat, oils, etc…</figcaption></figure><ul class="wp-block-list"><li>Try to replicate what you created at home as much as possible. Visual memory should help here with portion sizes, choices, etc… <strong>Use your hands to estimate!</strong></li><li>If you are trying to keep calories low, it might be a good idea to exaggerate the numbers in the meal in front of you, <strong>especially the fat count.</strong></li><li>Adjust the rest of the day around this event. Similarly to our <a href="/alcohol/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">alcohol guide</a>, you can try to get your protein in early in the day so you can be a bit more relaxed when going out. <em>Careful though,</em> making this a daily occurrence <strong>will</strong> most likely have an impact on your progress. <strong>Keep that in mind.</strong></li></ul><p><em><strong>“This is all great, Andy. But how do I count macros on things like burgers, pizza, etc…? Should I even bother?”</strong></em></p><p>Excellent question. Here’s another example:</p><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="942" height="942" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/RippedBody-Meal-Burger-Estimated.jpg" alt="" class="wp-image-58483" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/RippedBody-Meal-Burger-Estimated.jpg 942w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/RippedBody-Meal-Burger-Estimated-150x150.jpg 150w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/RippedBody-Meal-Burger-Estimated-450x450.jpg 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/RippedBody-Meal-Burger-Estimated-768x768.jpg 768w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/RippedBody-Meal-Burger-Estimated-150x150@2x.jpg 300w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/RippedBody-Meal-Burger-Estimated-450x450@2x.jpg 900w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 942px) 100vw, 942px"></figure><h4 class="wp-block-heading">How can we estimate those macros?</h4><p>Just like we have done at home, all we have to do is break it down into smaller steps.</p><p>The more you cook at home, the more you’ll learn about these things, and the easier it will be to estimate them for you.</p><ul class="wp-block-list"><li><strong>Look at the menu.</strong> A burger is simply meat, bread, cheese and whatever condiments the restaurant chose to make it tasty. With that in mind, our first step is to try and learn the quantities that the restaurant serves. This can be easily done by paying attention to the menu in front of your eyes, it usually states the weight of the meat and a list of the ingredients used to create your burger.</li><li><strong>Use your simplified rules. </strong>If you see “150g of pure Angus beef” on the menu, create a rule for yourself on how much fat content that type of meat has. Once you have created that rule, use it everywhere you go, it will keep things simpler and will help you stay consistent.</li><li><strong>Choose wisely. </strong>Ask for sauces to be served separately so you can control how much you add. They are usually mayo-based, so go with the macros of mayonnaise here.</li><li><strong>Add it all together. </strong>After deciding what you want and the simplified rules you are applying, you could use an online calculator to add all the ingredients up once, and see what the numbers approximately are. If you are cutting and want to be on the safe side, overestimate the fat macros by 5-10%. Again, be consistent here; remember that this is not a daily occurrence, so we are trying to make things as simple as possible.</li></ul><p><strong>Here’s how I simplified my calculations for the burger above, note that I added no sauce at all (I asked for it to be removed) and used my 15% fat content rule with the meat. </strong></p><p>The meat could very well be 30% or 8% fat, but I will not <a href="https://rippedbody.com/stress/" data-wpil-monitor-id="222">stress</a> about it since I’m trying to be as consistent as possible and can always adjust at a later stage if I see my numbers are all over the place, or I’m not making the progress I had anticipated.</p><p>I suggest you do something similar and make it as easy as possible to not stress yourself when eating out.</p><p><em>Prioritize cooking at home, and you will learn the proper skills to navigate your life outside the comfort of your kitchen.</em></p><p><a rel="noreferrer noopener" href="https://www.eatthismuch.com" target="_blank"><strong>EatThisMuch.com</strong></a> is also a great tool to plan out meals.</p><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity is-style-wide"><h2 class="wp-block-heading has-text-align-center" id="faq">Macro Counting Q&A</h2><div class="schema-faq wp-block-yoast-faq-block"><div class="schema-faq-section" id="faq-question-1596705898305"><strong class="schema-faq-question">How do I figure out my macros?</strong><p class="schema-faq-answer">Use a macro calculator, like <a href="https://rippedbody.com/macro-calculator/">The RippedBody Macro Calculator</a>, to figure out your macros for your body type and goals in 60 seconds.</p></div><div class="schema-faq-section" id="faq-question-1596705909980"><strong class="schema-faq-question">How do I calculate my macros for weight loss?</strong><p class="schema-faq-answer">Just use my <a href="https://rippedbody.com/macro-calculator/">macro calculator</a>.</p></div><div class="schema-faq-section" id="faq-question-1596705919771"><strong class="schema-faq-question">What is a macro?</strong><p class="schema-faq-answer">Macros refer to the three macronutrients: <strong>carbohydrate, protein, and fat</strong>.<br><br>Calorie intake determines whether weight is gained or lost, but the macronutrient content of the calories you eat has a significant effect on whether that change is fat or muscle mass, how you feel, perform, and how easy your nutrition plan is to stick to. This guide teaches you how to make meals out of your macros once you have <a href="https://rippedbody.com/macro-calculator/">calculated</a> them.</p></div><div class="schema-faq-section" id="faq-question-1596705931993"><strong class="schema-faq-question">What do you eat on a macro diet?</strong><p class="schema-faq-answer">A macro diet should be rich in fruits, vegetables, have sufficient protein (<a href="https://rippedbody.com/macros/">1 g/lb of body weight per day</a>), and be predominantly fresh and unprocessed foods. It is the same as any other “healthy” diet; the difference is that people have daily carbohydrate, protein, and fat intake targets which affect the specific food choices. This guide is here to teach you how to build meals out of your macros.</p></div><div class="schema-faq-section" id="faq-question-1596705940978"><strong class="schema-faq-question">What should I do if I can’t count macros?</strong><p class="schema-faq-answer">It’s possible to get shredded without counting anything if you are prepared to repeat the same meal combinations. I had two very successful cuts before I knew what I was doing, using that method. Here’s how it’s done:<br><br>• Cook most of your meals at home. Be sure you are consuming protein at each meal.<br>• Create a schedule based on your meal preference and stick to it.<br>• Track your body measurements and ensure your data is moving downwards.<br>• If you need to make a reduction, just remove a fist-sized portion of carbohydrates from your food each day. This should keep the scale moving in the right direction.<br>• Each time the fat loss stalls, remove another half-fist of carbohydrates from your meals. Do this as few times as you can, though, so that you’re eating as much as possible while still losing fat.<br><br><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="788" height="376" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2014/07/No-counting-tips-Site-Version-e1591492059174.jpg" class="attachment-full size-full" alt="" style="max-width: 100%; height: auto;" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2014/07/No-counting-tips-Site-Version-e1591492059174.jpg 788w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2014/07/No-counting-tips-Site-Version-e1591492059174-450x215.jpg 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2014/07/No-counting-tips-Site-Version-e1591492059174-768x366.jpg 768w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 788px) 100vw, 788px"><br><br>Using this method, technically your fat intake will remain pretty much unchanged throughout your cut, which isn’t optimal – carbs are important to fuel your training.</p></div><div class="schema-faq-section" id="faq-question-1596706111152"><strong class="schema-faq-question">How important is macro timing?</strong><p class="schema-faq-answer">Far less than was once thought. I have you covered in my article on <a href="https://rippedbody.com/nutrient-timing/">Nutrition Timing</a>.</p></div><div class="schema-faq-section" id="faq-question-1596706126337"><strong class="schema-faq-question">Why is it safe to not count most vegetables?</strong><p class="schema-faq-answer">Vegetables are not very energy-dense. With the exception of the few starchy ones (such as potatoes and carrots), it’s tough to eat so many that it makes a significant impact on your calorie intake.<br><br><em>•</em> 100 g<em> of raw tomatoes: ~3 g carbs, 1 g protein, ~=16 kcal</em><br>• <em>100 g of spinach: ~3 g carbs, 2 g protein, ~=20 kcal</em><br>• <em><strong>vs</strong></em> <em>100 g of butter =100 g fat, ~= 900 kcal</em><br><br>Even if you choose to eat a truly huge amount of vegetables each day to keep yourself full, 1.5 kg/3.3 lbs for example, and choose not to count any of it, at the worst case we’re only talking a ~300 kcal increase above what you were counting – if your digestive system were as efficient as a cow that is.<br><br>In reality, the energy availability of that veg will be lower than the standard 4 kcal per 1 g for carbs because it will be mostly fiber, which our bodies are not very good at taking the energy from. Also, it’s likely that your gut won’t be able to handle such a high amount of fiber anyway, and the severe bloating and/or diarrhea will get you to limit yourself naturally.<br><br>In a nutshell, fibrous (non-starchy) vegetables aren’t something we need to worry about counting. (My<br><a href="https://rippedbody.com/faq/">fiber and intake guidelines</a>.)</p></div><div class="schema-faq-section" id="faq-question-1596706246371"><strong class="schema-faq-question">Are large swings in calorie intake day to day fine if they lead to the same average intake over time?</strong><p class="schema-faq-answer">No. The bodyweight change would be the same in both situations, however, this isn’t optimal for workout recovery or nutrient partitioning. This is why binge-starve cycles don’t lead to ripped physiques.</p></div><div class="schema-faq-section" id="faq-question-1596706288304"><strong class="schema-faq-question">What about [insert food here]?</strong><p class="schema-faq-answer">Look it up in a calculator, make an executive decision on what you deem to be a reasonable simplification for this food, stick to it and that way you have the ‘consistency rule’ covered.</p></div><div class="schema-faq-section" id="faq-question-1596706318349"><strong class="schema-faq-question">I prefer to count everything. Is that ok?</strong><p class="schema-faq-answer">That can be absolutely fine. However, it’s also an indication of a slightly OCD personality, and for that reason, you may benefit from letting go to reduce stress.</p></div></div><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity"><p>I hope you found it useful. If anything doesn’t make sense just hit me up in the comments.</p><p>– Andy</p></div><form action="https://app.kit.com/forms/7749683/subscriptions" style="background-color:#fffefa;border-radius:10px" class="seva-form formkit-form" method="post" data-sv-form="7749683" data-uid="8462769b72" data-format="inline" data-version="5" min-width="400 500 600 700"><div data-style="full" style="--bg-border-radius:5px"><div data-element="column" class="formkit-column"><div class="formkit-header" style="color:#333333;font-weight:700" data-element="header"><h2><strong>STOP GUESSING. itemprop="name">Andy Morgan</h4> </a><div class="post-author-desc">Hi! The Ripped Body® Method is an evidence-based, data-driven approach to physique transformation.
⠀
For the majority of my 20s, I fell for the scams of the fitness industry. <img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="🤦🏻‍♂️" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f926-1f3fb-200d-2642-fe0f.svg">
⠀
This site is my revenge. <img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="😬" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f62c.svg">
⠀
Since 2011, I’ve written guides explaining how we get results to help readers avoid the same path of frustration. We’ve helped over 2,000 busy working guys transform their bodies WITHOUT supplements, fad diets, or the process dominating their lives.
⠀
<img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="📩" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f4e9.svg"> Enter your email address in the box at the bottom of this page and I'd be honored to show you how.</div></div></div></div></div><div class="wpdiscuz_top_clearing"></div><div id="comments" class="comments-area"><div id="respond" style="width: 0;height: 0;clear: both;margin: 0;padding: 0;"></div><div id="wpdcom" class="wpdiscuz_unauth wpd-default wpd-layout-1 wpd-comments-open"><div class="wc_social_plugin_wrapper"></div><div class="wpd-form-wrap"><div class="wpd-form-head"><div class="wpd-auth"><div class="wpd-login"></div></div></div><div class="wpd-form wpd-form-wrapper wpd-main-form-wrapper" id="wpd-main-form-wrapper-0_0"><form method="post" enctype="multipart/form-data" data-uploading="false" class="wpd_comm_form wpd_main_comm_form"><p style="font-size:20px; font-weight:900; text-transform:uppercase; margin-bottom:10px; margin-top:40px;">Got a question?     <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Thank you for this phenomenal article and truly spectacular website!</p>
<p>Would you be open to sharing your general thoughts on the healthfulness of some of the current diet trends like keto and carnivore?</p>
<p>Many people report weight loss, but I question the safety of such high fat – particularly saturated fat – diets.</p>
<p>I also wonder if those folks who exclude all plant foods can be getting all the nutrition they need from the few foods they do eat? (e.g. eating only meat, butter, bacon, and eggs)</p>
<p>I greatly appreciate your work!</p>

</div>

    <div class="wpd-comment-date" title="December 25, 2024 08:57">

</div>
    
    
    <div class="wpd-space"></div>
    <div class="wpd-comment-link wpd-hidden">
    <span wpd-tooltip="Comment Link" wpd-tooltip-position="left"><i class="fas fa-link" aria-hidden="true" data-wpd-clipboard="https://rippedbody.com/how-to-count-macros/#comment-90117"></i></span>

        Irwin M. Fletcher
    </a>
</div>
    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hi Irwin, thank you for the question and your patience.</p>
<p>1. <a href="https://rippedbody.com/problem-with-keto/" rel="ugc">The Problem With Keto</a>.</p>
<p>2. <strong>The Carnivore Diet in a Nutshell</strong><br>
80-90% of your foods should come from animal-based products, the majority of fruits, vegetables, and grains are toxic, and you must remain in ketosis (of course). It promises to help with “everything from weight loss, depression and anxiety, to autoimmune disease.” </p>
<p>If that sounds fucking ridiculous, you’d be right. If you hear someone say things along the lines of,</p>
<li>Seed oils are toxic,<br>
Saturated animal fat is fine in unlimited amounts,<br>
Dietary fiber is one big scam/conspiracy of the food industry (meaning, don’t bother eating fruits and vegetables),</li>
<p>…it’s this Carnivore diet arsehole of the diet internet where they are getting this from.</p>
<p>Remember, most people have lost the ability to see outside of their echo chamber, so unless they’re a loved one, I wouldn’t recommend you try to fight them on it ’cause you’re only going to facepalm yourself into oblivion trying.</p>
<p><strong>Here’s The Most Dangerous Bit</strong><br>
When people like me point out that a chronically high saturated fat intake is associated with a significantly raised risk of cardiovascular disease (CVD) and coronary heart disease (CHD), the common rebuttal is along the lines of, </p>
<blockquote><p>“Well, if even if LDL cholesterol spikes, you don’t have to worry about CVD/CHD because LDL is not causal. And if you’re otherwise metabolically healthy (lean, normal blood glucose levels, and low blood triglycerides), then actually, you have nothing to worry about.”</p></blockquote>
<p>But this is completely divorced from the abundance of evidence we have, and the worst thing is that the risk of CVD/CHD builds over time and cannot be reversed. So what you do now impacts your risk in 10-20 years. [​<a href="https://sigmanutrition.com/lipids/" target="_blank" rel="nofollow ugc">1</a>​, ​​<a href="https://sigmanutrition.com/diet-on-lipids/" target="_blank" rel="nofollow ugc">2</a>, ​<a href="https://sigmanutrition.com/diet-cvd/" target="_blank" rel="nofollow ugc">3</a>​]</p>
<p>It’s not going to be the proponents of this fad diet that will be held to account for this. Oh no, they’ll be long gone, having laughed their way to the bank. It’ll be you and your family that suffer the consequences.</p>
<p>So please, don’t get suckered by this bullshit.</p>
<p><strong>People To Avoid</strong><br>
The Carnivore diet’s father figure is someone that goes by ‘Dr. Paul Saladino’, or ‘Paul Saladino MD’.<br>
He trades heavily off of the fact he was interviewed on the Joe Rogan podcast. (Damn it, Joe!) Always a red flag. <img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="🚩" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f6a9.svg"> </p>
<p>Handily, if you go to his ​website​, just before you get to the photo of him shilling jars of the $50 beef organ supplements that you’ll conveniently (for his wallet) need to consume to make sure you have enough vitamin C to not get scurvy (I assume that’s his sales point). You’ll also see an “as seen on” section, which serves as a handy list of other “nutritional dipshits” to avoid.</p>

</div>

    </a>

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hi Shelly, thank you for the questions.</p>
<p>1. The more fat you have to lose, the less muscle you stand to lose when doing extreme things like this. However, I’d recommend against it, as it can lead to binge-starve cycles.</p>
<p>2. Nah, that’s fine. Let’s say you eat 150 g of protein in a day, spread across three meals. You have breakfast at 7, lunch at 12, you hit the gym at 3 pm, and you start boxing at 5. You’ll still be digesting your lunch by then, and your blood will be swimming with amino acids. So there’s really no need for the shake, assuming you’ll eat at 6 or 7 pm ish.</p>

</div>

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hi Andy – I train (fasted) pretty early in the AM and have found that I have more energy for harder workouts (legs, running, etc) if I have plenty of carbs the day before, which is often a rest or active recovery day. I will try your non-variable macros first… but curious if you have any guidance on something like this?</p>

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hello Andy</p>
<p>In certain products when I do the math between calories and macros, they don’t match. As an example to make myself clear:</p>
<p>In fatsecret, you search for chicken breast and the app says that it has the following macros and calories:</p>
<p>Protein: 23,09 g<br>
Carbs: 0 g<br>
Fat: 1, 24 g</p>
<p>Total calories: 110</p>
<p>However, if we do the math considering that carbs and protein have 4 calories per gram and fat 9 calories per gram, the resulting calories are 103.52 calories.</p>
<p>Where do the 6,48 extra calories come from?</p>
<p>I’m refering fatsecret here, but this is a common issue I’ve seen in many apps.</p>
<p>Thanks in advance for your answer.</p>

</div>

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hi Brooks, thank you for the question.</p>
<p>This comes up from time to time and I usually find people overestimate how difficult it can be.</p>
<p>200 g of protein can be as simple as having two large chicken breasts (one with lunch, one with dinner) and a couple of scoops of whey protein. It requires effort, but it not that hard. If you struggle though, you could always consider lowering your protein intake. I’ve talked about the cost-benefit/optimality point here: <a href="https://rippedbody.com/macros/">How To Set Your Macros for Fat Loss and Muscle Growth</a></p>

</div>

    <p>Hello Andy,</p>
<p>I’ve done 1 week of your Bodybuilding Novice and have been reading every materials on the websites.I’m an expat living and teaching in China. <p>I’m 40 years old, 183 cm and 86 kg.  The BMI shows I’m overweight.  I want to cut to 80 kg, or any recommendation can be helpful.  Your graph shows I would need about 180 g of protein.</p>

  function segmentSlugExists(slugArray) {

metadata.author: Andy Morgan
metadata.description: This guide to counting macros will teach you how to build meals out of foods you love so that you crush your physique goals. [Free calculator included.]
metadata.og:description: Updated: Nov.'17 - This visual guide to counting macros will teach you how to build meal plans out of foods that you love. Free macro calculator included.
metadata.og:title: Macro Counting 101: The Comprehensive, No-Nonsense Guide

data.title: How To Count Macros and Make Meals The Essential Guide
